behaviors.description: Renamed files appear in their own category and are not also counted as a deletion plus an addition.
behaviors.justification: Catching the real failure (tool doesn't detect renames) requires verifying the tool calls git with correct flags (e.g., `-M`), not just that parsing logic works. Mocking git output would miss command-construction bugs.
behaviors.test_description: Create a real git repository, stage a file rename, run the tool, and verify the file appears in the 'renamed' category only, with correct count, not duplicated in 'deleted' or 'added'.

behaviors.description: Diffs that include binary files do not cause the tool to crash; binary files are counted in the output.
behaviors.justification: Git's actual output for binary files (truncation, markers, special encoding) can only be tested with real subprocess calls. Mocking binary output might miss parsing edge cases.
behaviors.test_description: Initialize a real git repository, add actual binary files (e.g., PNG, PDF), commit changes, run the tool, and verify: (1) no crash, (2) binary files are counted in the appropriate change category.

behaviors.description: Running the tool on a repository that has no commits yet produces an empty report rather than an error.
behaviors.justification: The exact output or error from git on an empty repo must be tested with real git behavior. Mocking git output loses the nuances of how git responds in this edge case.
behaviors.test_description: Create a real git repository using 'git init' with no commits, run the tool, and verify it produces an empty report without errors or warnings.

behaviors.description: The counts in the report match the actual change history of the repository being analyzed.
behaviors.justification: This is the core correctness requirement. Verifying counts match actual history demands a real repository with known commits and real git commands. Mocking would hide bugs in parsing or category logic on real git output.
behaviors.test_description: Create a real git repository with a known sequence of commits (add N files, modify M files, delete K files, rename L files). Run the tool and verify the reported counts exactly match the actual changes without overlaps or omissions.

behaviors.description: The output is identical whether the changes being analyzed are staged or unstaged.
behaviors.justification: Real git state (staged vs. unstaged) must be tested with subprocess calls. The tool must call the correct git command(s) for each state. Mocking hides bugs in how the tool queries git.
behaviors.test_description: Create a real git repository, stage changes (add, modify, delete files), run the tool, record output. Unstage the same changes (or hard reset), run the tool again, and verify the output is identical in both cases.
